start_node.text: The class is looking at a crushed soda can. Student A says: 'I think the air just pushed it.' How do you respond?

choices.text: [REVOICING] 'So, you're saying the atmospheric pressure outside was stronger than the air inside?'
choices.tip: Great! Revoicing clarifies the student's thought for the whole class.

choices.text: [EVALUATION] 'Correct. It's called atmospheric pressure. Does everyone get that?'
choices.tip: The 'Evaluation' move ends the inquiry.

choices.text: [SAY MORE] 'That's an interesting start, Student A. Can you expand on what you mean by "pushed"?'
choices.tip: Nice! 'Say More' encourages the student to deepen their own critical thinking.

choices.text: [LEADING] 'It was the pressure, right? Like we talked about yesterday?'
choices.tip: Leading questions do the thinking for them.

turn_2.text: Student B chimes in: 'But wait, if air is pushing it, why doesn't air push us and crush us too?'

choices.text: [ADD ON] 'Who can build on Student B's question?'
choices.tip: Excellent! Adding on builds a collaborative environment.

choices.text: [EVALUATION] 'Because we have pressure inside us pushing out. Next question.'
choices.tip: Answering it yourself stops the students from thinking.

choices.text: [REASONING] 'Student A, how does Student B's point affect your initial idea?'
choices.tip: Perfect! Asking for reasoning pushes for deeper understanding.

choices.text: [COLD CALL] 'Student C, what's the answer?'
choices.tip: Cold calling without giving time to think can increase anxiety.

turn_3.text: Student C looks unsure. 'Maybe we are just stronger than the can?'

choices.text: [WAIT TIME] (Wait in silence for 5 seconds to let others process)
choices.tip: Silence is a tool! Give 3–5 seconds for processing.

choices.text: [PSEUDO-INQUIRY] 'Are we really stronger, or is it something else?'
choices.tip: Pseudo-inquiry hints too heavily at the answer.

choices.text: [REVOICING] 'So you're wondering if the material of the can versus human bodies makes the difference?'
choices.tip: Revoicing helps validate their contribution while clarifying it.

choices.text: [EVALUATION] 'No, that's not it. Think about the air inside us.'
choices.tip: Directly shutting down an idea lowers engagement.

turn_4.text: Student A suddenly raises a hand. 'Oh! We breathe air in! So there is air inside us pushing back out!'

choices.text: [REASONING] 'Why would air inside us pushing out prevent us from being crushed?'
choices.tip: Asking for the 'why' solidifies the concept.

choices.text: [EVALUATION] 'Exactly! Good job, Student A.'
choices.tip: Praise is nice, but it signals the thinking is done. Keep pushing!

choices.text: [ADD ON] 'Student B, does Student A's idea help answer your earlier question?'
choices.tip: Connecting student ideas is a master-level move.

choices.text: [LEADING] 'So the pressures are equal, right?'
choices.tip: You gave them the punchline! Let them say it.

turn_5.text: Student B nods slowly. 'Yeah... if the push from the inside matches the push from the outside, they cancel out.'

choices.text: [SAY MORE] 'Cancel out? Can you say more about what that looks like?'
choices.tip: Asking them to elaborate ensures they aren't just using buzzwords.

choices.text: [REVOICING] 'So the internal and external pressures are in equilibrium?'
choices.tip: Good, introducing scientific vocabulary (equilibrium) by revoicing.

choices.text: [EVALUATION] 'Yep, they cancel out. Moving on.'
choices.tip: Rushing to move on kills the momentum.

choices.text: [COLD CALL] 'Student C, repeat what Student B just said.'
choices.tip: Asking for mere repetition isn't as engaging as asking for their thoughts on it.

turn_6.text: Student C speaks up. 'But the can was empty. There was no air inside to push back out.'

choices.text: [REASONING] 'Is a seemingly empty can truly empty? What do you all think?'
choices.tip: Great pivot to address a misconception.

choices.text: [EVALUATION] 'Actually, it was full of air until we heated it and sealed it.'
choices.tip: You explained the experiment instead of letting them figure it out.

choices.text: [ADD ON] 'Who can respond to Student C's observation?'
choices.tip: Tossing the question back to the class builds student autonomy.

choices.text: [LEADING] 'Wasn't there water vapor inside that condensed?'
choices.tip: Too leading! You're doing the heavy lifting.

turn_7.text: Student A says, 'Oh! We put a little water in it and boiled it. The steam pushed the air out!'

choices.text: [WAIT TIME] (Pause to let the class absorb this realization)
choices.tip: Giving them time to process a big realization is crucial.

choices.text: [REVOICING] 'So the steam replaced the air, and then what happened when we cooled it?'
choices.tip: Revoicing and prompting the next logical step.

choices.text: [EVALUATION] 'Right, and then the steam condensed, creating a vacuum.'
choices.tip: You stole their thunder by finishing the explanation!

choices.text: [PSEUDO-INQUIRY] 'And then it condensed, didn't it?'
choices.tip: A fill-in-the-blank question doesn't require deep thought.

turn_8.text: Student B concludes: 'When it cooled, the steam turned back to water. It took up less space, so the pressure inside dropped, and the outside air crushed it!'

choices.text: [ADD ON] 'Does everyone agree with this summary? Thumbs up or down.'
choices.tip: A great way to check for consensus and finalize the learning.

choices.text: [REASONING] 'Why does steam turning back to water cause the pressure to drop?'
choices.tip: Pushing for that final layer of molecular understanding.

choices.text: [EVALUATION] 'Perfect. That's the end of the lesson.'
choices.tip: A bit abrupt, but they got there.

choices.text: [LEADING] 'So the outside pressure was greater, right?'
choices.tip: Ending on a leading question diminishes their hard work.
